div.p: Alexander by the grace of God king of the Scots sends greeting to all worthy men of the whole of his land. Know us to have allowed, and by this our charter to have made firm, the sale that Patrick earl of Dunbar, by the agreement of Patrick his son and heir, made to the abbot and convent of Melrose of his whole horse-stud that he had in the feu of Lauder in the Year of Grace One Thousand Two Hundred and Forty-Seven on the feast of the Beheading of John the Baptist. And we have likewise allowed, and by this our charter made firm, the concession and quitclaim that the same earl, with the agreement of the said Patrick his son and heir, made to the same abbot and convent for the common that he had for feeding or pasturing the horse-stud or any other animals within the lands and pastures which the said abbot and convent held and possessed on the said day in the said feu of Lauder, that is, so the said earl or his heirs or anyone else in their name shall in future instigate or have no claim or complaint in the said lands or pastures; which very sale and quitclaim we will to be observed for ever, just as the charter of the foresaid Patrick earl of Dunbar which the said abbot and convent have thereupon rightly bears witness. As witnesses, William Comyn, earl of Menteith; Alan Durward, justiciar of Scotia; Robert of Roos; David of Lindsay; Thomas de Normanville; Thomas son of Ranulph; Robert Menzies. At Holyrood on the twenty-eighth day of November, in the thirty-third year of the reign of the lord King. [1247]
